project_overview.project_development_objective: To provide access to electricity or other social and economic infrastructure services to communities in the project area in order to strengthen community support for CASA-1000 transmission line
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 700 communities spread over 23 districts in 6 provinces (Parwan, Kunduz, Baghlan, Laghman, Nangarhar, Kabul) with a total of over 152,000 families along the 4 km corridor of influence (COI)

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Afghanistan is described as an extremely fragile state emerging from over three decades of conflict. The project specifically addresses implementation in High Risk Areas (HRAs) with security classifications ranging from 'relatively secure to mildly insecure' to 'extremely insecure'. Some districts are considered insecure with deteriorating security environment throughout the country

key_components.description: Provides grants directly to communities to fund economic infrastructure sub-projects with preference for power-sector projects (micro hydro power, solar panels, expansion of power lines). Grant amount calculated at US$200 per family with maximum ceiling of US$30,000 per community. Communities can use grants for other infrastructure if power already exists or is not technically feasible

Engages communities through Facilitating Partners to build capacity and ownership. Includes participatory planning, community governance, conflict resolution, community participatory monitoring, bookkeeping, contracting management, and communication with government and contractors. Covers FP costs including basic facilitation and allowances for insecurity, remoteness, and seasonality

Covers operational expenditures (8% of total grant) and third-party monitoring (US$1.5 million). Includes monitoring of FP engagement, financial monitoring of grants, physical and quality checks of sub-project implementation. Uses NSP implementation arrangements and systems

Finances strong communications campaign and information-sharing activities to relevant stakeholders with emphasis on communities in project areas. Multi-media communications strategy including mobile cinemas, radio, TV, newspapers, face-to-face meetings. Ensures feedback loops through perception surveys, community participatory monitoring, and grievance handling mechanisms
Contingency funds to cover any increase in number of communities due to finalization of transmission line route, and to cover additional communities in High Risk Areas to address equity issues

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Rural Rehabilitation and Development (MRRD) through the National Solidarity Program (NSP) Project Management Office (PMO)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Three-level implementation structure: (1) National level - MRRD provides oversight, PMO manages implementation, inter-ministerial Steering Committee chaired by Second Vice President; (2) Provincial level - Provincial Implementation Units (PIUs) monitor Facilitating Partners; (3) Village level - Community Development Councils (CDCs) implement sub-projects. Regular coordination meetings between CASA-1000 contractors, CSP Facilitating Partners, MoEW, and MRRD/NSP. MoU in place between MRRD and MoEW for rural electrification
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: National Competitive Bidding (NCB) for goods and works using World Bank Standard Bidding Documents. Facilitating Partners selected through Fixed Budget Selection (FBS) or Single Source Selection (SSS). Third Party Monitoring firm selected through Quality and Cost Based Selection (QCBS). Community procurement follows Community Driven Development approach using NSP Community Procurement Manual
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Multi-tier monitoring system including: (1) Management Information System (MIS) for tracking all activities; (2) Implementation Monitoring (IM) by trained officers in provinces; (3) Post-Implementation Monitoring (PIM) on sample basis 6 months after grant utilization; (4) Community Participatory Monitoring (CPM) teams in communities; (5) Third Party Monitoring for sample-based verification of FP engagement, financial monitoring, and quality checks; (6) Grievance handling database; (7) Quarterly Interim Financial Reports; (8) Annual audited financial statements by Supreme Audit Office

identified_risks.risk_description: Afghanistan is an extremely fragile state emerging from over three decades of conflict with ongoing security challenges. Political and economic uncertainties including unexpected government leadership changes and decrease in donor funds create difficulties for project implementation and supervision
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project uses High Risk Area Strategy (HRAS) developed under NSP with flexibility in implementation using 'distance model', additional allowances for insecure areas, guidelines for engaging women, use of local and third party monitors. Security assessments conducted every 6 months. Project based on well-tried NSP implementation modalities to minimize design, FM, procurement, and safeguard risks

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of communities outside the CoI not covered under CSP being dissatisfied with frustrated expectations. Sustainability of sub-projects depends on improved technical designs, implementation quality, and continued operation and maintenance by communities. Country suffers from lack of engineers with strong competition among donors for limited qualified personnel
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Communication Plan (Component 4) to link CSP to communities within CoI and underline differences from NSP. Flexibility in selection of communities outside COI, especially for insecure areas. Improved technical capacity through training of field staff and FP staff. O&M committees established in each CDC with training on O&M. Engineers and monitors conduct regular field visits to ensure fee collection mechanisms for O&M are in place

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk that communities will not be able to derive power directly from transmission line despite it passing through their areas, potentially leading to local frustration and targeting of project by parties with ulterior motives
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project specifically designed to address this through CSP grants for electricity and other infrastructure. Communications strategy emphasizes benefits and manages expectations. Community ownership created through participatory planning and implementation. Grievance redress mechanisms in place

identified_risks.risk_description: MRRD/NSP capacity to manage additional workload from CSP implementation alongside NSP III operations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Additional staff will be hired to serve as focal points in PMU and provincial levels. Project uses existing NSP systems and arrangements already in place. NSP has transformed from expatriate-run to locally-managed entity with strong FM, procurement, and monitoring capacity. Adequate FM staff in place with FMA support

identified_risks.risk_description: Overall FM risk rating is High due to country context, but residual risk after mitigation is Substantial. Challenges in maintaining fiduciary standards in weak institutional environment
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM arrangements follow existing NSP arrangements with Financial Management Agent (GiZ) providing oversight. Adequate FM staff at central and provincial levels. MIS system and Quick Books accounting software in place with capability to segregate receipts and payments by funding source. Float accounts managed by FMA with dual signatories. Internal audit by MRRD and NSP internal control units. Supreme Audit Office conducts annual audits with Audit Agent support. Quarterly IFRs submitted within 45 days. FM manual being updated by August 31, 2014

identified_risks.risk_description: Procurement risk in weak institutional environment with limited capacity in line ministries and local private sector. Lack of ownership and procurement champions in government
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: NSP has satisfactory procurement performance record. Procurement Department staffed with national specialist and qualified staff. Contract management system deployed for FP contracts. Prior review by Bank for contracts above thresholds. Post-procurement audit annually. Procurement Plans updated annually. Two implementation support missions per annum. Procurement Risk Mitigation Monitoring Plan in place with specific performance targets

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited involuntary land acquisition may be needed for sub-projects. Environmental impacts from small-scale infrastructure works including air, water, noise pollution from excavation and quarry operations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) from NSP III adapted for CSP. Operational Policy 4.12 triggered with procedures for willing buyer-willing seller, voluntary donations, or mutually agreeable compensation. Resettlement Policy Framework developed under CASA-1000 adapted for CSP. Independent third party monitoring of ESMF implementation. Environmental category B with mitigation measures in Environmental Management Plan. Standard technical designs for MHP with environmental, health, safety mitigations. Safeguard officers provide training to project personnel

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: B - Limited adverse environmental impacts from small-scale infrastructure works
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Grievance handling database established with categories for CDC Mobilization, CDC Election, CDP, Procurement, Financial, Engineering, Social, Process delays, Quality, Coverage, and Land acquisition. Grievance Redressal Mechanism (GRM) in place with target of 80% of grievances addressed. Community Participatory Monitoring teams provide feedback mechanisms. Complaints Handling Unit (CHU) established
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender mainstreamed throughout project. Proactive measures to ensure women's active participation in trainings, consultations, decision-making, and monitoring. CDC elections provide for representation of women with three options: (1) Mixed gender CDCs; (2) Executive Body with Sub-committees with equal male/female members; (3) Male CDC with Women's Sub-committee. At least 50% of total beneficiaries targeted to be female. Women's participation in sub-project site selection required with signatures from two female CDC/women's committee members. Gender strategy based on NSP lessons learned. Female literacy rate in project area estimated at 21% with only 36% overall literacy
vulnerable_groups: Ethnic minorities - seven main ethnic groups and six main tribes along COI
Communities in High Risk Areas with insecurity, remoteness, and seasonality challenges

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity in mind. Rationale includes: (1) Creating local ownership to mitigate risk of transmission line being targeted by parties with ulterior motives; (2) Addressing equity issues between communities immediately affected by line (who cannot access power directly) and larger society that benefits; (3) Managing local frustration at inability to access power from line. Communications strategy emphasizes managing expectations and building goodwill. Community participatory monitoring and grievance mechanisms provide feedback loops. Project uses NSP's High Risk Area Strategy (HRAS) with flexibility for insecure areas
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Afghanistan is described as 'extremely fragile state' emerging from over three decades of conflict. Key fragility factors: (1) GNI per capita of $570 - lowest income country in South Asia; (2) High poverty at 36% with unemployment at 8% projected to rise; (3) Deteriorating security environment with likelihood of activity suspension if insecurity increases; (4) Weak institutional capacity outside Kabul with lack of qualified civil service; (5) Large illegal and illicit economy; (6) Dependence on foreign aid ($16 billion per annum) set to decline with withdrawal of international forces; (7) Projected economic growth decline from 9% to 5-6% over 2011-18; (8) Limited access to electricity (30% in urban areas, 9% in rural areas) as development constraint
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security situation in Afghanistan makes any operation highly risky. Project specifically addresses security through: (1) High Risk Area Strategy (HRAS) classifying districts as Secure, Partially Insecure, Highly Insecure, or Extremely Insecure on 6-monthly basis; (2) Flexibility in implementation using 'distance model' with greater reliance on community members; (3) Additional allowances for work in highly insecure and extremely insecure communities; (4) Guidelines on engaging women in insecure areas; (5) Use of local and third party monitors; (6) Limitations on conducting regular field visits due to security restrictions addressed through Third Party Monitoring; (7) Potential for some activities to be suspended if insecurity increases
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Project area includes significant displaced and refugee populations. Vulnerable group data shows: (1) Returnees - 16,034 families (11% of total population along COI); (2) Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) - 8,237 families (6% of total). These groups are specifically identified as vulnerable and targeted for inclusion in project benefits. Resettlement Policy Framework developed under CASA-1000 adapted for CSP to address any land acquisition needs. Procedures for willing buyer-willing seller, voluntary donations, or mutually agreeable compensation in place. Land acquisition exceeding 100 sqm or individual livelihood impact exceeding 10% requires formal procedures
